perturbation.original_text: The Executive is entitled to 25 days' paid holiday in addition to the usual public or bank holidays in England) in every Holiday Year, to be taken at times convenient to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Executive is entitled to paid holiday in addition to the usual public or bank holidays in England) in every Holiday Year, to be taken at times convenient to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the '25 days' of paid holiday, the contract introduces an in-text contradiction. Later calculations regarding holiday pay are based on a specific number of days. This creates ambiguity and uncertainty about the amount of holiday the executive is actually entitled to, creating an internal contradiction because Clause 11.3 refers to holiday amount while now that ammount is undefined in Clause 11.1
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Company reserves the right to require the Executive to take any outstanding holiday during any period of notice of termination of employment or to make a payment in lieu of holiday outstanding at the Termination Date. If, at the Termination Date, the Executive has taken more holiday than he has accrued, the Executive hereby expressly consents to the Company deducting an appropriate amount from any payments otherwise due him. Deductions and payments in lieu of holiday are to be calculated on the basis that a day's holiday is equal to 1/260 of the Executive's basic salary.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies '25 days' paid holiday, which is essential for calculating deductions and payments in lieu of holiday as stated in the contradicted text. By removing '25 days', the changed text creates an in-text contradiction because the '1/260 of the Executive's basic salary' calculation relies on a defined number of holiday days, which is now missing. This ambiguity directly impacts how holiday pay is determined, making the contract inconsistent and potentially unenforceable. Thus, a strong contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 8.1 The Company operates a Group pension plan (the Plan). The Executive is entitled to participate in the Plan (or such pension scheme as may be established by the Company to replace the Plan), subject to its trust deeds and rules from time to time. The Executive has opted out of the Plan. Whilst his status remains so, he will receive in lieu the annual sum of 10% of his current salary, paid monthly in instalments, together with his salary.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1 The Company operates a Group pension plan (the Plan). The Executive is entitled to participate in the Plan (or such pension scheme as may be established by the Company to replace the Plan), subject to its trust deeds and rules from time to time. The Executive has opted out of the Plan. Whilst his status remains so, he will receive a sum in lieu together with his salary.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clear definition of the amount to be received in lieu 10% of his current salary, the contract introduces an in-text contradiction. The removal of the precise percentage creates an inconsistency because the modified text no longer specifies how the sum in lieu is calculated creating an ambiguity about the amount the executive is supposed to be getting paid.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.1 The Company operates a Group pension plan (the Plan). The Executive is entitled to participate in the Plan (or such pension scheme as may be established by the Company to replace the Plan), subject to its trust deeds and rules from time to time. The Executive has opted out of the Plan. Whilst his status remains so, he will receive a sum in lieu together with his salary.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the specific detail of '10% of his current salary' from the compensation details. The contradicted text then includes the changed text, so it is contradictory to the original intent of the document. The original text gives a specific amount, while the changed text is vague. This vagueness causes a strong contradiction because the executive's payment is no longer clearly defined within the document. Therefore, it creates an inconsistency and contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 The Company will pay the Executive a salary of £740,000 and a fixed benefits allowance of £30,000 per annum. The salary (and so far, as is reasonably possible) the benefits allowance will accrue from day to day and be payable in equal instalments in arrears on or around the 25th day of every month, less deductions for income tax and National Insurance contributions and shall be inclusive of any fees receivable by the Executive as a director of any Group Company.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 The Company will pay the Executive a salary and a benefits allowance per annum. The salary (and so far, as is reasonably possible) the benefits allowance will accrue from day to day and be payable in equal instalments in arrears on or around the 25th day of every month, less deductions for income tax and National Insurance contributions and shall be inclusive of any fees receivable by the Executive as a director of any Group Company.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exact amount of salary to be paid (£740,000) and the fixed benefits allowance of (£30,000), the contract introduces an in-text contradiction. By deleting the specific amounts, there are no specific amount for each to be paid creating an ambiguity about the amount the executive is supposed to be getting paid.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.1 The Company will pay the Executive a salary and a benefits allowance per annum. The salary (and so far, as is reasonably possible) the benefits allowance will accrue from day to day and be payable in equal instalments in arrears on or around the 25th day of every month, less deductions for income tax and National Insurance contributions and shall be inclusive of any fees receivable by the Executive as a director of any Group Company.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the specified salary (£740,000) and benefits allowance (£30,000). This omission creates a direct contradiction because without these specific amounts, the contract is ambiguous and fails to define the key financial obligations to the executive, creating an in-text contradiction according to the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition. The contradicted text expects specific figures for salary and benefits, while the changed text provides none, rendering it impossible to fulfill the original expectation within the contract.
